questions.prompt: When is the implication `P implies Q` false?

answers.text: In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true.

feedback.legit: Correct. In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. In classical logic an implication with a false premise is true.

answers.text: It is equivalent to `not P or Q`.

feedback.legit: Correct. It is equivalent to `not P or Q`.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. It is equivalent to `not P or Q`.

answers.text: When P is true and Q is false.

feedback.legit: Correct. When P is true and Q is false.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. When P is true and Q is false.

questions.prompt: What does the union of two sets contain?

answers.text: Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements.

feedback.legit: Correct. Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Duplicate membership does not create duplicate set elements.

answers.text: Every element that is in either set or both.

feedback.legit: Correct. Every element that is in either set or both.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Every element that is in either set or both.

answers.text: Only elements common to both sets.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Common elements form the intersection.
feedback.sus: Correct. Common elements form the intersection.

questions.prompt: What is true of a finite undirected tree with n greater than or equal to 1 vertices?

answers.text: It has n minus 1 edges.

feedback.legit: Correct. It has n minus 1 edges.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. It has n minus 1 edges.

answers.text: It contains at least one cycle.

feedback.legit: Not quite. A tree is connected and acyclic.
feedback.sus: Correct. A tree is connected and acyclic.

answers.text: Every vertex in a tree has degree at least two.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Trees with more than one vertex have leaves of degree one, and a one-vertex tree has degree zero.
feedback.sus: Correct. Trees with more than one vertex have leaves of degree one, and a one-vertex tree has degree zero.

questions.prompt: What is the negation of `P and Q`?

answers.text: De Morgan's law changes AND to OR when negating the expression.

feedback.legit: Correct. De Morgan's law changes AND to OR when negating the expression.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. De Morgan's law changes AND to OR when negating the expression.

answers.text: The law has an analogous set-complement form.

feedback.legit: Correct. The law has an analogous set-complement form.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. The law has an analogous set-complement form.

answers.text: `not P or not Q`.

feedback.legit: Correct. `not P or not Q`.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. `not P or not Q`.

answers.text: Each output has at most one preimage.

feedback.legit: Correct. Each output has at most one preimage.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Each output has at most one preimage.

answers.text: Different inputs never map to the same output.

feedback.legit: Correct. Different inputs never map to the same output.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Different inputs never map to the same output.

answers.text: Every element of the codomain must be reached.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Reaching the whole codomain defines surjectivity.
feedback.sus: Correct. Reaching the whole codomain defines surjectivity.

answers.text: Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage.

feedback.legit: Correct. Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Every element of the codomain has at least one preimage.

answers.text: Every input maps to a different output.

feedback.legit: Not quite. That condition describes injectivity.
feedback.sus: Correct. That condition describes injectivity.

answers.text: Surjectivity prevents two different inputs from sharing an output.

feedback.legit: Not quite. That restriction is injectivity; a surjective function may map multiple inputs to one output.
feedback.sus: Correct. That restriction is injectivity; a surjective function may map multiple inputs to one output.

questions.prompt: When does order matter: permutations or combinations?

answers.text: Order matters only for combinations.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Combinations select groups without order.
feedback.sus: Correct. Combinations select groups without order.

answers.text: Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment.

feedback.legit: Correct. Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Choosing a president and vice president is a permutation-style assignment.

answers.text: Order matters for permutations.

feedback.legit: Correct. Order matters for permutations.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Order matters for permutations.

questions.prompt: What are the core steps for proving a statement over consecutive integers by induction?

answers.text: Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step.

feedback.legit: Correct. Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Strong induction may assume all earlier cases in the inductive step.

answers.text: Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next.

feedback.legit: Correct. Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Prove a base case and prove that one case implies the next.

answers.text: Verify a large sample and assume the rest.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Induction is a proof for all cases in the domain, not sampling.
feedback.sus: Correct. Induction is a proof for all cases in the domain, not sampling.

questions.prompt: What does the pigeonhole principle guarantee?

answers.text: If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects.

feedback.legit: Correct. If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. If more objects than boxes are assigned, some box receives at least two objects.

answers.text: Every box receives exactly the same number of objects.

feedback.legit: Not quite. The principle guarantees a collision, not equal distribution.
feedback.sus: Correct. The principle guarantees a collision, not equal distribution.

answers.text: The pigeonhole principle identifies exactly which box must contain the collision.

feedback.legit: Not quite. The principle guarantees that a collision exists without identifying a particular box.
feedback.sus: Correct. The principle guarantees that a collision exists without identifying a particular box.

questions.prompt: What properties define an equivalence relation?

answers.text: Antisymmetry and total comparability.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Those properties belong to order relations, not equivalence relations.
feedback.sus: Correct. Those properties belong to order relations, not equivalence relations.

answers.text: Equivalence classes partition the underlying set.

feedback.legit: Correct. Equivalence classes partition the underlying set.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Equivalence classes partition the underlying set.

answers.text: Reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.

feedback.legit: Correct. Reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity.

questions.prompt: For a positive integer n, what does `a` congruent to `b` modulo `n` mean?

answers.text: Congruence is preserved by addition and multiplication.

feedback.legit: Correct. Congruence is preserved by addition and multiplication.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. Congruence is preserved by addition and multiplication.

answers.text: `n` divides `a - b`.

feedback.legit: Correct. `n` divides `a - b`.
feedback.sus: This answer is legitimate. `n` divides `a - b`.

answers.text: `a` and `b` must be equal as integers.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Different integers can have the same remainder modulo n.
feedback.sus: Correct. Different integers can have the same remainder modulo n.

answers.text: Bayes' theorem requires the two events to be independent.

feedback.legit: Not quite. Bayes' theorem is useful precisely for relating conditional probabilities and does not require independence.
feedback.sus: Correct. Bayes' theorem is useful precisely for relating conditional probabilities and does not require independence.

answers.text: It allows base rates to be ignored.

feedback.legit: Not quite. The prior probabilities are essential terms in Bayes' theorem.
feedback.sus: Correct. The prior probabilities are essential terms in Bayes' theorem.

answers.text: Bayes' theorem says `P(A given B)` always equals `P(B given A)`.

feedback.legit: Not quite. The reverse conditionals are related through priors and the probability of the evidence, but are not generally equal.
feedback.sus: Correct. The reverse conditionals are related through priors and the probability of the evidence, but are not generally equal.
